author: Leo the Great
note: The classic Western statement of full deity AND full humanity in the one incarnate Son: 'whole in what was His own, whole in what was ours.' The Tome was read and acclaimed at the Council of Chalcedon (451): 'Peter has spoken through Leo.' Feltoe NPNF wording (task's paraphrase 'true God was born in the complete and perfect nature of true man' differs from the printed text quoted here). Verify verbatim against source_url before publishing. Textbook Chalcedonian/devotional; no clause requires fencing. Verified verbatim against three independent PD hosts of the Feltoe NPNF2-12 text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); source_url confirmed correct.
text: Thus in the whole and perfect nature of true man was true God born, complete in what was His own, complete in what was ours.

author: Leo the Great
note: The incarnation-for-redemption passage: 'majesty took on humility... inviolable nature was united with possible nature' so the one Mediator 'could both die with the one and not die with the other.' DELICACY: the NPNF Feltoe printed text here reads 'possible nature'; the theological sense is 'passible' (the impassible divine nature united to the passible human nature) — this is a known peculiarity of the printed NPNF wording. Quoted verbatim as printed; do not silently 'correct' to 'passible' without flagging. Verify verbatim against source_url before publishing. DELICACY: (1) 'possible nature' is CONFIRMED as what the printed NPNF/Feltoe actually prints - a printing quirk for 'passible' (capable of suffering). KEEP it verbatim; when quoting on a page an editorial gloss '[sic: passible]' is appropriate, because 'possible nature' is close to meaningless to a modern reader and invites the misreading 'a nature that was merely possible'. (2) 'could both die with the one and not die with the other' is hearable as two subjects (Nestorian) if lifted alone - it must travel with the in-quote fences 'one and the same Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus' and 'which then came together in one person.' Verified verbatim against three independent PD hosts of the Feltoe NPNF2-12 text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); source_url confirmed correct.
text: Without detriment therefore to the properties of either nature and substance which then came together in one person, majesty took on humility, strength weakness, eternity mortality: and for the paying off of the debt belonging to our condition inviolable nature was united with possible nature, so that, as suited the needs of our case, one and the same Mediator between God and men, the Man Christ Jesus, could both die with the one and not die with the other.

author: Leo the Great
note: The two natures acting each according to its own property within the one Person (communicatio idiomatum): the Word performing what is the Word's, the flesh what is the flesh's. Feltoe NPNF wording ('For each form does what is proper to it with the co-operation of the other') differs from the task's paraphrase ('each nature does what is proper to it in communion with the other'). Verify verbatim against source_url before publishing. Textbook Chalcedonian/devotional; no clause requires fencing. Verified verbatim against three independent PD hosts of the Feltoe NPNF2-12 text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); source_url confirmed correct.
text: For each form does what is proper to it with the co-operation of the other; that is the Word performing what appertains to the Word, and the flesh carrying out what appertains to the flesh.

author: Leo the Great
note: One Person of God and man, in whom humiliation and glory are alike shared, though each arises from a distinct source (the humanity, the deity). DELICACY: read in isolation, 'the source of the degradation' could be misheard as subordinationism, and 'one person... God and man' as Nestorian two-subjects — but Leo's point is precisely the opposite: the single subject, the one Christ, to whom both the lowly and the glorious acts belong. Frame as Chalcedonian one-person Christology when quoting. Verify verbatim against source_url before publishing. DELICACY: 'the source of the degradation... is one, and the source of the glory... is another' is hearable as two subjects (Nestorian) or as subordination if lifted alone. It is fenced in-quote by 'in the Lord Jesus Christ God and man is one person'. CRITICAL: preserve the leading 'For although...' - truncating to the 'yet' clause destroys the fence. Verified verbatim against three independent PD hosts of the Feltoe NPNF2-12 text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); source_url confirmed correct.
text: For although in the Lord Jesus Christ God and man is one person, yet the source of the degradation, which is shared by both, is one, and the source of the glory, which is shared by both, is another.

author: Leo the Great
work: Sermon 21 (On the Feast of the Nativity, I)
note: Leo's most-quoted Christmas exhortation: because God took our nature, the redeemed Christian bears a new dignity and must not fall back into 'the old baseness.' Year is approximate (Leo's Nativity sermons span his pontificate, c. 440-461). DELICACY: 'becoming a partner in the Divine nature' echoes 2 Peter 1:4 (theosis / partaking of the divine nature by grace) — orthodox, but a compressed reader could hear 'becoming God' ontologically; it means participation in God's life by grace through union with Christ, not equality with God. Frame accordingly. Verify verbatim against source_url before publishing. DELICACY: 'becoming a partner in the Divine nature' (2 Pet 1:4) is hearable as deification-as-equality. It is fenced in-quote by 'Remember the Head and the Body of which thou art a member' (participation by union, not identity) and 'thou wert rescued from the power of darkness' (received by grace, not achieved). Denominationally safe - 2 Pet 1:4 is common ground. 'wert' (not 'wast') is confirmed as printed. Verified verbatim against three independent PD hosts of the Feltoe NPNF2-12 text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); source_url confirmed correct.
text: Christian, acknowledge thy dignity, and becoming a partner in the Divine nature, refuse to return to the old baseness by degenerate conduct. Remember the Head and the Body of which thou art a member. Recollect that thou wert rescued from the power of darkness and brought out into God's light and kingdom.

author: Leo the Great
work: Sermon 21 (On the Feast of the Nativity, I)
note: The opening line of Leo's first Nativity sermon — a warm, universally-received incarnational note for devotional/Christmas use. Only this opening sentence is captured here verbatim; extend the quotation only after confirming the following lines against source_url. Year approximate. Verify verbatim against source_url before publishing. Textbook Chalcedonian/devotional; no clause requires fencing. Verified verbatim against three independent PD hosts of the Feltoe NPNF2-12 text (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20); source_url confirmed correct.
text: Our Saviour, dearly-beloved, was born today: let us be glad.
